text.KJV: And in as much as not without an othe he was made Priest,
text.NABRE: And to the degree that this happened not without the taking of an oath —for others became priests without an oath,

text.NRSV: but this one became a priest with an oath, because of the one who said to him, “The Lord has sworn and will not change his mind, ‘You are a priest forever.’”
text.Rotherham: For, they, indeed, apart from oath-taking, have been made priests, but, he, with an oath-taking, through him that was saying unto him—The Lord sware, and will not regret,—Thou, art a priest, age-abidingly
text.DRB: But this with an oath, by him that said unto him: The Lord hath sworn, and he will not repent, Thou art a priest for ever.)
text.KJV: (For those Priests were made without an oath: but this with an oath, by him þ said vnto him, The Lord sware and wil not repent, thou art a Priest for euer after the order of Melchisedec)
text.NABRE: but he with an oath, through the one who said to him: “The Lord has sworn, and he will not repent: ‘You are a priest forever’”—

text.NRSV: accordingly Jesus has become the guarantee of a better covenant.
text.Rotherham: By as much as this, hath, Jesus, become surety of a better covenant also.
text.DRB: By so much is Jesus made a surety of a better testament.
text.KJV: By so much was Iesus made a suertie of a better Testament.
text.NABRE: to that same degree has Jesus [also] become the guarantee of an [even] better covenant.

text.NRSV: Furthermore, the former priests were many in number, because they were prevented by death from continuing in office;
text.Rotherham: And, they, indeed, in greater numbers, have been made priests, because, by reason of death, are they hindered from remaining at hand;
text.DRB: And the others indeed were made many priests , because by reason of death they were not suffered to continue:
text.KJV: And they truely were many Priests, because they were not suffered to continue by reason of death.
text.NABRE: Those priests were many because they were prevented by death from remaining in office,

text.NRSV: but he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever.
text.Rotherham: But, he, by reason of his remaining age-abidingly, untransmissible, holdeth, the priesthood.
text.DRB: But this, for that he continueth for ever, hath an everlasting priesthood,
text.KJV: But this man because hee continueth euer, hath an vnchangeable Priesthood.
text.NABRE: but he, because he remains forever, has a priesthood that does not pass away.

text.NRSV: Consequently he is able for all time to save those who approach God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them.
text.Rotherham: Whence he is able, even to be saving unto the very end, them who approach, through him, unto God; Since he evermore liveth to be interceding in their behalf.
text.DRB: Whereby he is able also to save for ever them that come to God by him; always living to make intercession for us.
text.KJV: Wherefore he is able also to saue them to the vttermost, that come vnto God by him, seeing hee euer liueth to make intercession for them.
text.NABRE: Therefore, he is always able to save those who approach God through him, since he lives forever to make intercession for them.

text.NRSV: For it was fitting that we should have such a high priest, holy, blameless, undefiled, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens.
text.Rotherham: For, such a high-priest as this, for us, was [even] suited: Loving, noble, undefiled, set apart from sinners, and become, higher than the heavens;
text.DRB: For it was fitting that we should have such a high priest, holy, innocent, undefiled, separated from sinners, and made higher than the heavens;
text.KJV: For such an high Priest became vs, who is holy, harmelesse, vndefiled, separate from sinners, and made higher then the heauens.
text.NABRE: It was fitting that we should have such a high priest: holy, innocent, undefiled, separated from sinners, higher than the heavens.

text.NRSV: Unlike the other high priests, he has no need to offer sacrifices day after day, first for his own sins, and then for those of the people; this he did once for all when he offered himself.
text.Rotherham: Who hath no daily necessity, like the high-priests, beforehand, over his own sins, to be offering sacrifices, after that, over those, of the people,—for, this, he did once for all when, himself, he offered up.
text.DRB: Who needeth not daily (as the other priests) to offer sacrifices first for his own sins, and then for the people's: for this he did once, in offering himself.
text.KJV: Who needeth not daily, as those high Priests, to offer vp sacrifice, first for his owne sins and then for the peoples: for this he did once, when he offered vp himselfe.
text.NABRE: He has no need, as did the high priests, to offer sacrifice day after day, first for his own sins and then for those of the people; he did that once for all when he offered himself.

text.NRSV: For the law appoints as high priests those who are subject to weakness, but the word of the oath, which came later than the law, appoints a Son who has been made perfect forever.
text.Rotherham: For, the law, constituteth, men, high-priests, having, weakness; but, the word of the oath-taking, which cometh after the law, A Son, age-abidingly, made perfect.
text.DRB: For the law maketh men priests, who have infirmity: but the word of the oath, which was since the law, the Son who is perfected for evermore.
text.KJV: For the Law maketh men high Priests which haue infirmitie, but the word of the othe which was since the Law, maketh the Sonne, who is consecrated for euermore.
text.NABRE: For the law appoints men subject to weakness to be high priests, but the word of the oath, which was taken after the law, appoints a son, who has been made perfect forever.

text.NRSV: Now the main point in what we are saying is this: we have such a high priest, one who is seated at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens,
text.Rotherham: A crowning point on the things being spoken:—such a one as this, have we, as high-priest, who hath sat down on the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens,—
text.DRB: Now of the things which we have spoken, this is the sum: We have such an high priest, who is set on the right hand of the throne of majesty in the heavens,
text.KJV: Now of the things which we haue spoken, this is the summe: wee haue such an high Priest, who is set on the right hand of the throne of the Maiestie in the heauens:
text.NABRE: Chapter 8 Heavenly Priesthood of Jesus. The main point of what has been said is this: we have such a high priest, who has taken his seat at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven,

text.NRSV: a minister in the sanctuary and the true tent that the Lord, and not any mortal, has set up.
text.Rotherham: Of the Holy place, a public minister, and of the Real Tent, which, the Lord, pitched and not man.
text.DRB: A minister of the holies , and of the true tabernacle, which the Lord hath pitched, and not man.
text.KJV: A minister of the Sanctuary, and of the true Tabernacle, which the Lord pitched, and not man.
text.NABRE: a minister of the sanctuary and of the true tabernacle that the Lord, not man, set up.

text.NRSV: For every high priest is appointed to offer gifts and sacrifices; hence it is necessary for this priest also to have something to offer.
text.Rotherham: For, every high-priest, for the offering of both gifts and sacrifices, is constituted; whence it was necessary for, this one also, to have something which he might offer.
text.DRB: For every high priest is appointed to offer gifts and sacrifices: wherefore it is necessary that he also should have some thing to offer.
text.KJV: For euery high Priest is ordeined to offer gifts and sacrifices: wherefore it is of necessitie that this man haue somewhat also to offer.
text.NABRE: Now every high priest is appointed to offer gifts and sacrifices; thus the necessity for this one also to have something to offer.

text.NRSV: Now if he were on earth, he would not be a priest at all, since there are priests who offer gifts according to the law.
text.Rotherham: If, indeed, therefore, he had been on earth, he had not, in that case, even been a priest, since there are those who are offering the gifts, according to the law:—
text.DRB: If then he were on earth , he would not be a priest: seeing that there would be others to offer gifts according to the law,
text.KJV: For if he were on earth, he should not bee a Priest, seeing that there are Priests that offer gifts according to the Law:
text.NABRE: If then he were on earth, he would not be a priest, since there are those who offer gifts according to the law.

text.NRSV: They offer worship in a sanctuary that is a sketch and shadow of the heavenly one; for Moses, when he was about to erect the tent, was warned, “See that you make everything according to the pattern that was shown you on the mountain.”
text.Rotherham: Who, indeed, are rendering divine service, with a glimpse and shadow, of the heavenly things; even as Moses hath received intimation, when about to complete the tent,—For see! saith he—Thou shalt make all things according to the model which hath been pointed out to thee in the mount.
text.DRB: Who serve unto the example and shadow of heavenly things. As it was answered to Moses, when he was to finish the tabernacle: See (saith he) that thou make all things according to the pattern which was shewn thee on the mount.
text.KJV: Who serue vnto the example and shadow of heauenly things, as Moses was admonished of God when he was about to make the Tabernacle. For see (saith he) that thou make all things according to the paterne shewed to thee in the mount.
text.NABRE: They worship in a copy and shadow of the heavenly sanctuary, as Moses was warned when he was about to erect the tabernacle. For he says, “See that you make everything according to the pattern shown you on the mountain.”

text.NRSV: But Jesus has now obtained a more excellent ministry, and to that degree he is the mediator of a better covenant, which has been enacted through better promises.
text.Rotherham: But, now, hath he attained unto, a more distinguished public ministry,—by as much as of a better covenant also he is, mediator, which indeed, upon better promises, hath been legislated.
text.DRB: But now he hath obtained a better ministry, by how much also he is a mediator of a better testament, which is established on better promises.
text.KJV: But now hath he obtained a more excellent ministerie, by how much also he is the Mediatour of a better Couenant, which was established vpon better promises.
text.NABRE: Now he has obtained so much more excellent a ministry as he is mediator of a better covenant, enacted on better promises. Old and New Covenants.

text.NRSV: For if that first covenant had been faultless, there would have been no need to look for a second.
text.Rotherham: For, if, that first, had been, faultless, not, in that case, for a second, had there been sought, a place.
text.DRB: For if that former had been faultless, there should not indeed a place have been sought for a second.
text.KJV: For if that first Couenant had bene faultles, then should no place haue bene sought for the second.
text.NABRE: For if that first covenant had been faultless, no place would have been sought for a second one.

text.NRSV: God finds fault with them when he says: “The days are surely coming, says the Lord, when I will establish a new covenant with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah;
text.Rotherham: For, finding fault with them, he saith—Lo! days are coming, saith the Lord, when I will conclude, for the house of Israel and the house of Judah, a covenant of a new sort:
text.DRB: For finding fault with them, he saith: Behold, the days shall come, saith the Lord: and I will perfect unto the house of Israel, and unto the house of Juda, a new testament:
text.KJV: For finding fault with them, hee saith, Behold, the dayes come (saith the Lord) when I will make a new couenant with the house of Israel, and the house of Iudah.
text.NABRE: But he finds fault with them and says: “Behold, the days are coming, says the Lord, when I will conclude a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah.

text.NRSV: not like the covenant that I made with their ancestors on the day when I took them by the hand to lead them out of the land of Egypt; for they did not continue in my covenant, and so I had no concern for them, says the Lord.
text.Rotherham: Not according to the covenant which I made with their fathers, in the day when I took them by their hand, to lead them forth out of the land of Egypt,—because, they, abode not in my covenant, and, I, disregarded them, saith the Lord.
text.DRB: Not according to the testament which I made to their fathers, on the day when I took them by the hand to lead them out of the land of Egypt: because they continued not in my testament: and I regarded them not, saith the Lord.
text.KJV: Not according to the Couenant that I made with their fathers, in the day when I tooke them by the hand to lead them out of the land of Egypt, because they continued not in my Couenant, and I regarded them not, saith the Lord.
text.NABRE: It will not be like the covenant I made with their fathers the day I took them by the hand to lead them forth from the land of Egypt; for they did not stand by my covenant and I ignored them, says the Lord.

text.NRSV: This is the covenant that I will make with the house of Israel after those days, says the Lord: I will put my laws in their minds, and write them on their hearts, and I will be their God, and they shall be my people.
text.Rotherham: Because, this, is the covenant which I will make with the house of Israel, after those days, saith the Lord:—giving my laws into their understanding, upon their hearts also, will I inscribe them: and I will become their God, and, they, shall become my people;
text.DRB: For this is the testament which I will make to the house of Israel after those days, saith the Lord: I will give my laws into their mind, and in their heart will I write them: and I will be their God, and they shall be my people:
text.KJV: For this is the Couenant that I will make with the house of Israel after those dayes, saith the Lord: I wil put my Lawes into their minde, and write them in their hearts: and I will be to them a God, and they shalbe to me a people.
text.NABRE: But this is the covenant I will establish with the house of Israel after those days, says the Lord: I will put my laws in their minds and I will write them upon their hearts. I will be their God, and they shall be my people.

text.NRSV: And they shall not teach one another or say to each other, ‘Know the Lord,’ for they shall all know me, from the least of them to the greatest.
text.Rotherham: And in nowise shall they teach—every one his fellow-citizen, and every one his brother, saying,—Get to know the Lord! Because, all, shall know me, from the least unto the greatest of them;
text.DRB: And they shall not teach every man his neighbour and every man his brother, saying, Know the Lord: for all shall know me from the least to the greatest of them:
text.KJV: And they shall not teach euery man his neighbour, and euery man his brother, saying, Know the Lord: For all shall know me, from the least to the greatest.
text.NABRE: And they shall not teach, each one his fellow citizen and kinsman, saying, ‘Know the Lord,’ for all shall know me, from least to greatest.

text.NRSV: For I will be merciful toward their iniquities, and I will remember their sins no more.”
text.Rotherham: Because, propitious, will I be as to their unrighteousnesses, and, of their sins, in nowise will I be mindful, any more.
text.DRB: Because I will be merciful to their iniquities, and their sins I will remember no more.
text.KJV: For I will be mercifull to their vnrighteousnes, and their sins & their iniquities will I remember no more.
text.NABRE: For I will forgive their evildoing and remember their sins no more.”

text.NRSV: In speaking of “a new covenant,” he has made the first one obsolete. And what is obsolete and growing old will soon disappear.
text.Rotherham: In saying, Of a new sort, he hath made obsolete, the first; but, the thing that is becoming obsolete and aged, is near, disappearing!
text.DRB: Now in saying a new , he hath made the former old. And that which decayeth and groweth old, is near its end.
text.KJV: In that he saith, A new Couenant, he hath made the first olde. Now that which decayeth and waxeth old, is readie to vanish away.
text.NABRE: When he speaks of a “new” covenant, he declares the first one obsolete. And what has become obsolete and has grown old is close to disappearing.

text.NRSV: Now even the first covenant had regulations for worship and an earthly sanctuary.
text.Rotherham: [Even] the first, indeed, therefore, used to have righteous appointments of divine service, even the holy ritual well arranged.
text.DRB: The former indeed had also justifications of divine service, and a worldly sanctuary.
text.KJV: Then verily the first Couenant had also ordinances of diuine Seruice, and a worldly Sanctuary.
text.NABRE: Chapter 9 The Worship of the First Covenant. Now [even] the first covenant had regulations for worship and an earthly sanctuary.

text.NRSV: For a tent was constructed, the first one, in which were the lampstand, the table, and the bread of the Presence; this is called the Holy Place.
text.Rotherham: For a tent was prepared, the first, in which were both the lampstand and the table and the setting forth of the loaves,—the which is called the Holy place;
text.DRB: For there was a tabernacle made the first, wherein were the candlesticks, and the table, and the setting forth of loaves, which is called the holy.
text.KJV: For there was a Tabernacle made, the first, wherein was the Candlesticke, and the Table, and the Shewbread, which is called the Sanctuarie.
text.NABRE: For a tabernacle was constructed, the outer one, in which were the lampstand, the table, and the bread of offering; this is called the Holy Place.

text.NRSV: Behind the second curtain was a tent called the Holy of Holies.
text.Rotherham: But, after the second veil, a tent, that which is called Most Holy:
text.DRB: And after the second veil, the tabernacle, which is called the holy of holies:
text.KJV: And after the second vaile, the Tabernacle which is called þ Holiest of all:
text.NABRE: Behind the second veil was the tabernacle called the Holy of Holies,

text.NRSV: In it were the golden altar of incense and the ark of the covenant overlaid on all sides with gold, in which there were a golden urn holding the manna, and Aaron’s rod that budded, and the tablets of the covenant;
text.Rotherham: Having a golden altar of incense, and the ark of the covenant covered around on every side with gold, in which was a golden jar holding the manna, and the rod of Aaron that sprouted, and the tables of the covenant;
text.DRB: Having a golden censer, and the ark of the testament covered about on every part with gold, in which was a golden pot that had manna, and the rod of Aaron, that had blossomed, and the tables of the testament.
text.KJV: Which had the golden Censor, and the Arke of the Couenant ouerlayed round about with gold, wherein was the Golden pot that had Manna, and Aarons rod that budded, and the Tables of the Couenant.
text.NABRE: in which were the gold altar of incense and the ark of the covenant entirely covered with gold. In it were the gold jar containing the manna, the staff of Aaron that had sprouted, and the tablets of the covenant.

text.NRSV: above it were the cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat. Of these things we cannot speak now in detail.
text.Rotherham: But, over-above it, Cherubim of glory overshadowing the propitiatory:—concerning which things, it is not now [needful] to be speaking, particularly.
text.DRB: And over it were the cherubims of glory overshadowing the propitiatory: of which it is not needful to speak now particularly.
text.KJV: And ouer it the Cherubims of glory shadowing the Mercyseat; of which we cannot now speake particularly.
text.NABRE: Above it were the cherubim of glory overshadowing the place of expiation. Now is not the time to speak of these in detail.

text.NRSV: Such preparations having been made, the priests go continually into the first tent to carry out their ritual duties;
text.Rotherham: Now, these things having been thus prepared, into the first tent, indeed, continually do the priests enter, the divine services completing;
text.DRB: Now these things being thus ordered, into the first tabernacle the priests indeed always entered, accomplishing the offices of sacrifices.
text.KJV: Now when these things were thus ordained, the Priestes went alwayes into the first Tabernacle, accomplishing the seruice of God.
text.NABRE: With these arrangements for worship, the priests, in performing their service, go into the outer tabernacle repeatedly,

text.NRSV: but only the high priest goes into the second, and he but once a year, and not without taking the blood that he offers for himself and for the sins of the people committed in ignorance.
text.Rotherham: But, into the second, once for all in the year, only the high-priest, not without blood, which he offered for himself and the ignorances of the people:
text.DRB: But into the second, the high priest alone, once a year: not without blood, which he offereth for his own, and the people's ignorance:
text.KJV: But into the second went the high Priest alone once euery yeere, not without blood, which he offered for himselfe, and for the errors of the people.
text.NABRE: but the high priest alone goes into the inner one once a year, not without blood that he offers for himself and for the sins of the people.

text.NRSV: By this the Holy Spirit indicates that the way into the sanctuary has not yet been disclosed as long as the first tent is still standing.
text.Rotherham: The Holy Spirit making this evident—that, not yet, hath been manifested, the way through the Holy place, so long as the first tent hath a standing.
text.DRB: The Holy Ghost signifying this, that the way into the holies was not yet made manifest, whilst the former tabernacle was yet standing.
text.KJV: The holy Ghost this signifying, that the way into the Holiest of all, was not yet made manifest, while as the first Tabernacle was yet standing:
text.NABRE: In this way the holy Spirit shows that the way into the sanctuary had not yet been revealed while the outer tabernacle still had its place.

text.NRSV: This is a symbol of the present time, during which gifts and sacrifices are offered that cannot perfect the conscience of the worshiper,
text.Rotherham: The which is a similitude for the present season, according to which both gifts and sacrifices are offered, which cannot, as to the conscience, perfect him that rendereth the divine service:—
text.DRB: Which is a parable of the time present: according to which gifts and sacrifices are offered, which can not, as to the conscience, make him perfect that serveth, only in meats and in drinks,
text.KJV: Which was a figure for the time then present, in which were offred both gifts and sacrifices, that could not make him that did the seruice perfect, as pertayning to the conscience,
text.NABRE: This is a symbol of the present time, in which gifts and sacrifices are offered that cannot perfect the worshiper in conscience

text.NRSV: but deal only with food and drink and various baptisms, regulations for the body imposed until the time of renewal comes.
text.Rotherham: Only as to eatings, and drinkings, and diversified immersions,—righteous-appointments of the flesh, which, until a season of rectifying, are in force.
text.DRB: And divers washings, and justices of the flesh laid on them until the time of correction .
text.KJV: Which stood onely in meates and drinkes, and diuers washings, and carnall ordinances imposed on them vntill the time of reformation.
text.NABRE: but only in matters of food and drink and various ritual washings: regulations concerning the flesh, imposed until the time of the new order.

text.NRSV: But when Christ came as a high priest of the good things that have come, then through the greater and perfect tent (not made with hands, that is, not of this creation),
text.Rotherham: But, when Christ approached, as high-priest of the coming good things, through the greater and more perfect tent, not made by hand, that is, not of this creation,—
text.DRB: But Christ, being come an high priest of the good things to come, by a greater and more perfect tabernacle not made with hand, that is, not of this creation:
text.KJV: But Christ being come an high Priest of good things to come, by a greater and more perfect Tabernacle, not made with hands, that is to say, not of this building:
text.NABRE: Sacrifice of Jesus. But when Christ came as high priest of the good things that have come to be, passing through the greater and more perfect tabernacle not made by hands, that is, not belonging to this creation,

text.NRSV: he entered once for all into the Holy Place, not with the blood of goats and calves, but with his own blood, thus obtaining eternal redemption.
text.Rotherham: Nor yet through blood of goats and calves, but through his own blood he entered once for all into the Holy place, age-abiding redemption discovering.
text.DRB: Neither by the blood of goats, or of calves, but by his own blood, entered once into the holies, having obtained eternal redemption .
text.KJV: Neither by the blood of Goats and Calues: but by his owne blood hee entred in once into the Holy place, hauing obtained eternall redemption for vs.
text.NABRE: he entered once for all into the sanctuary, not with the blood of goats and calves but with his own blood, thus obtaining eternal redemption.

text.NRSV: For if the blood of goats and bulls, with the sprinkling of the ashes of a heifer, sanctifies those who have been defiled so that their flesh is purified,
text.Rotherham: For if the blood of goats and bulls, and the ashes of a heifer sprinkling the profaned, halloweth unto the purity of the flesh,
text.DRB: For if the blood of goats and of oxen, and the ashes of an heifer being sprinkled, sanctify such as are defiled, to the cleansing of the flesh:
text.KJV: For if the blood of Bulls, and of goats, and the ashes of an heifer sprinkling the vncleane, sanctifieth to the purifying of the flesh:
text.NABRE: For if the blood of goats and bulls and the sprinkling of a heifer’s ashes can sanctify those who are defiled so that their flesh is cleansed,

text.NRSV: how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience from dead works to worship the living God!
text.Rotherham: How much rather shall the blood of the Christ, who through an age-abiding spirit offered himself unspotted unto God, purify our conscience from dead works, to the rendering of divine-service, unto a Living God?
text.DRB: How much more shall the blood of Christ, who by the Holy Ghost offered himself unspotted unto God, cleanse our conscience from dead works, to serve the living God?
text.KJV: How much more shall the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit, offered himselfe without spot to God, purge your conscience from dead workes, to serue the liuing God?
text.NABRE: how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal spirit offered himself unblemished to God, cleanse our consciences from dead works to worship the living God.

text.NRSV: For this reason he is the mediator of a new covenant, so that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance, because a death has occurred that redeems them from the transgressions under the first covenant.
text.Rotherham: And, for this cause, of a new covenant, is he mediator,—to the end that, death coming to pass for the redemption of the transgressions against the first covenant, the called might receive the promise of the age-abiding inheritance;
text.DRB: And therefore he is the mediator of the new testament: that by means of his death, for the redemption of those transgressions, which were under the former testament, they that are called may receive the promise of eternal inheritance.
text.KJV: And for this cause hee is the Mediatour of the New Testament, that by meanes of death, for the redemption of the transgressions that were vnder the first Testament, they which are called, might receiue the promise of eternall inheritance.
text.NABRE: For this reason he is mediator of a new covenant: since a death has taken place for deliverance from transgressions under the first covenant, those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance.

text.NRSV: Where a will is involved, the death of the one who made it must be established.
text.Rotherham: For, where a covenant is, it is necessary for, the death, to be brought in, of him that hath covenanted;
text.DRB: For where there is a testament, the death of the testator must of necessity come in.
text.KJV: For where a Testament is, there must also of necessitie bee the death of the Testatour.
text.NABRE: Now where there is a will, the death of the testator must be established.

text.NRSV: For a will takes effect only at death, since it is not in force as long as the one who made it is alive.
text.Rotherham: For, a covenant over dead persons, is firm,—since it is not then of force when he is living that hath covenanted.
text.DRB: For a testament is of force, after men are dead: otherwise it is as yet of no strength, whilst the testator liveth.
text.KJV: For a Testament is of force after men are dead: otherwise it is of no strength at all whilest the Testatour liueth.
text.NABRE: For a will takes effect only at death; it has no force while the testator is alive.

text.NRSV: Hence not even the first covenant was inaugurated without blood.
text.Rotherham: Whence, not even the first, apart from blood, hath been consecrated;
text.DRB: Whereupon neither was the first indeed dedicated without blood.
text.KJV: Whereupon, neither the first Testament was dedicated without blood.
text.NABRE: Thus not even the first covenant was inaugurated without blood.

text.NRSV: For when every commandment had been told to all the people by Moses in accordance with the law, he took the blood of calves and goats, with water and scarlet wool and hyssop, and sprinkled both the scroll itself and all the people,
text.Rotherham: For, when every commandment according to the law had been spoken by Moses unto all the people, taking the blood of the calves and the goats, with water and scarlet wool and hyssop, both, the scroll itself, and all the people, he sprinkled;
text.DRB: For when every commandment of the law had been read by Moses to all the people, he took the blood of calves and goats, with water, and scarlet wool and hyssop, and sprinkled both the book itself and all the people,
text.KJV: For when Moses had spoken euery precept to all the people according to the Law, he tooke the blood of Calues and of Goates, with water and scarlet wooll, and hysope, and sprinckled both the booke and all the people,
text.NABRE: When every commandment had been proclaimed by Moses to all the people according to the law, he took the blood of calves [and goats], together with water and crimson wool and hyssop, and sprinkled both the book itself and all the people,

text.NRSV: saying, “This is the blood of the covenant that God has ordained for you.”
text.Rotherham: Saying—This, is the blood of the covenant which, God, hath sent in command unto you:
text.DRB: Saying: This is the blood of the testament, which God hath enjoined unto you.
text.KJV: Saying, This is the blood of the Testament which God hath enioyned vnto you.
text.NABRE: saying, “This is ‘the blood of the covenant which God has enjoined upon you.’”

text.NRSV: And in the same way he sprinkled with the blood both the tent and all the vessels used in worship.
text.Rotherham: Yea, the tent also, and all the utensils of the public ministry, with blood, in like manner, he sprinkled:
text.DRB: The tabernacle also and all the vessels of the ministry, in like manner, he sprinkled with blood.
text.KJV: Moreouer, hee sprinkled with blood both the Tabernacle, and all the vessels of the Ministery.
text.NABRE: In the same way, he sprinkled also the tabernacle and all the vessels of worship with blood.

text.NRSV: Indeed, under the law almost everything is purified with blood, and without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.
text.Rotherham: And, nearly, all things, with blood, are purified, according to the law, and, apart from blood-shedding, cometh no remission.
text.DRB: And almost all things, according to the law, are cleansed with blood: and without shedding of blood there is no remission.
text.KJV: And almost all things are by the Law purged with blood: and without shedding of blood is no remission.
text.NABRE: According to the law almost everything is purified by blood, and without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness.

text.NRSV: Thus it was necessary for the sketches of the heavenly things to be purified with these rites, but the heavenly things themselves need better sacrifices than these.
text.Rotherham: It was indeed therefore necessary for the glimpses of the things in the heavens with these to be purified; but, the heavenly things themselves, with better sacrifices than these.
text.DRB: It is necessary therefore that the patterns of heavenly things should be cleansed with these: but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these.
text.KJV: It was therefore necessary that the patterns of things in the heauens should bee purified with these, but the heauenly things themselues with better sacrifices then these.
text.NABRE: Therefore, it was necessary for the copies of the heavenly things to be purified by these rites, but the heavenly things themselves by better sacrifices than these.

text.NRSV: For Christ did not enter a sanctuary made by human hands, a mere copy of the true one, but he entered into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf.
text.Rotherham: For, not into a Holy place made by hand, entered Christ,—counterpart of the real [Holy place] ; but, into the heaven itself, now, to be plainly manifested before the face of God in our behalf;—
text.DRB: For Jesus is not entered into the holies made with hands, the patterns of the true: but into heaven itself, that he may appear now in the presence of God for us.
text.KJV: For Christ is not entred into the Holy places made with handes, which are the figures of the true, but into heauen it selfe, now to appeare in the presence of God for vs.
text.NABRE: For Christ did not enter into a sanctuary made by hands, a copy of the true one, but heaven itself, that he might now appear before God on our behalf.

text.NRSV: Nor was it to offer himself again and again, as the high priest enters the Holy Place year after year with blood that is not his own;
text.Rotherham: Nor yet that, ofttimes, he should be offering himself,—just as the high-priest entereth into the Holy place, year by year, with alien blood;—
text.DRB: Nor yet that he should offer himself often , as the high priest entereth into the holies, every year with the blood of others:
text.KJV: Nor yet that he should offer himselfe often, as the high Priest entreth into the Holy place, euery yeere with blood of others:
text.NABRE: Not that he might offer himself repeatedly, as the high priest enters each year into the sanctuary with blood that is not his own;

text.NRSV: for then he would have had to suffer again and again since the foundation of the world. But as it is, he has appeared once for all at the end of the age to remove sin by the sacrifice of himself.
text.Rotherham: Else had it been needful for him, ofttimes, to suffer, from the foundation of the world; but, now, once for all, upon a conjunction of the ages, for a setting aside of sin through means of his sacrifice, hath he been made manifest;
text.DRB: For then he ought to have suffered often from the beginning of the world: but now once at the end of ages, he hath appeared for the destruction of sin, by the sacrifice of himself.
text.KJV: For then must hee often haue suffered since the foundation of the world: but now once in the end of the world, hath he appeared to put away sinne by the sacrifice of himselfe.
text.NABRE: if that were so, he would have had to suffer repeatedly from the foundation of the world. But now once for all he has appeared at the end of the ages to take away sin by his sacrifice.

text.NRSV: And just as it is appointed for mortals to die once, and after that the judgment,
text.Rotherham: And, inasmuch as it is in store for men—once for all to die, but after this, judgment,
text.DRB: And as it is appointed unto men once to die, and after this the judgment:
text.KJV: And as it is appointed vnto men once to die, but after this the Iudgement:
text.NABRE: Just as it is appointed that human beings die once, and after this the judgment,

text.NRSV: so Christ, having been offered once to bear the sins of many, will appear a second time, not to deal with sin, but to save those who are eagerly waiting for him.
text.Rotherham: Thus, the Christ also, once for all having been offered, for the bearing of the sins, of many, a second time, apart from sin, will appear, to them who for him are ardently waiting—unto salvation.
text.DRB: So also Christ was offered once to exhaust the sins of many; the second time he shall appear without sin to them that expect him unto salvation.
text.KJV: So Christ was once offered to beare the sinnes of many, & vnto them that looke for him shall hee appeare the second time without sinne, vnto saluation.
text.NABRE: so also Christ, offered once to take away the sins of many, will appear a second time, not to take away sin but to bring salvation to those who eagerly await him.

text.NRSV: Since the law has only a shadow of the good things to come and not the true form of these realities, it can never, by the same sacrifices that are continually offered year after year, make perfect those who approach.
text.Rotherham: For the law, having in a shadow of the destined good things, not the very image of the things, they can never, with the same sacrifices which year by year they offer evermore, make them who approach, perfect;
text.DRB: For the law having a shadow of the good things to come, not the very image of the things; by the selfsame sacrifices which they offer continually every year, can never make the comers thereunto perfect:
text.KJV: For the Law hauing a shadow of good things to come, and not the very Image of the things, can neuer with those sacrifices which they offered yeere by yeere continually, make the commers thereunto perfect:
text.NABRE: Chapter 10 One Sacrifice Instead of Many. Since the law has only a shadow of the good things to come, and not the very image of them, it can never make perfect those who come to worship by the same sacrifices that they offer continually each year.

text.NRSV: Otherwise, would they not have ceased being offered, since the worshipers, cleansed once for all, would no longer have any consciousness of sin?
text.Rotherham: Else would they not, in that case, have ceased being offered, by reason of those rendering the divine service having no further conscience at all of sins, being once for all purified?
text.DRB: For then they would have ceased to be offered: because the worshippers once cleansed should have no conscience of sin any longer:
text.KJV: For then would they not haue ceased to be offered, because that the worshippers once purged, should haue had no more conscience of sinnes?
text.NABRE: Otherwise, would not the sacrifices have ceased to be offered, since the worshipers, once cleansed, would no longer have had any consciousness of sins?

text.NRSV: But in these sacrifices there is a reminder of sin year after year.
text.Rotherham: But, in them, is a recalling to mind of sins, year by year,
text.DRB: But in them there is made a commemoration of sins every year.
text.KJV: But in those sacrifices there is a remembrance againe made of sinnes euery yeere.
text.NABRE: But in those sacrifices there is only a yearly remembrance of sins,

text.NRSV: For it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins.
text.Rotherham: For it is impossible for blood of bulls and goats to be taking away sins.
text.DRB: For it is impossible that with the blood of oxen and goats sin should be taken away.
text.KJV: For it is not possible that the blood of Bulles and of Goats, should take away sinnes.
text.NABRE: for it is impossible that the blood of bulls and goats take away sins.

text.NRSV: Consequently, when Christ came into the world, he said, “Sacrifices and offerings you have not desired, but a body you have prepared for me;
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, coming into the world, he saith: Sacrifice and offering, thou willedst not, but, a body, hast thou fitted for me,—
text.DRB: Wherefore when he cometh into the world, he saith: Sacrifice and oblation thou wouldest not: but a body thou hast fitted to me:
text.KJV: Wherefore when hee commeth into the world, he saith, Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not, but a body hast thou prepared mee:
text.NABRE: For this reason, when he came into the world, he said: “Sacrifice and offering you did not desire, but a body you prepared for me;

text.NRSV: in burnt offerings and sin offerings you have taken no pleasure.
text.Rotherham: In whole-burnt-offerings and sacrifices for sins, thou didst not delight:
text.DRB: Holocausts for sin did not please thee.
text.KJV: In burnt offerings, and sacrifices for sinne thou hast had no pleasure:
text.NABRE: holocausts and sin offerings you took no delight in.

text.NRSV: Then I said, ‘See, God, I have come to do your will, O God’ (in the scroll of the book it is written of me).”
text.Rotherham: Then, said I—Lo! I am come,—in the heading of the scroll, it is written concerning me,—to do, O God, thy will.
text.DRB: Then said I: Behold I come: in the head of the book it is written of me: that I should do thy will, O God.
text.KJV: Then said I, Loe, I come. (In the volume of the booke it is written of me) to doe thy will, O God.
text.NABRE: Then I said, ‘As is written of me in the scroll, Behold, I come to do your will, O God.’”

text.NRSV: When he said above, “You have neither desired nor taken pleasure in sacrifices and offerings and burnt offerings and sin offerings” (these are offered according to the law),
text.Rotherham: Higher up, saying—Sacrifices, and offerings, and whole-burnt-offerings, and sacrifices for sins, thou willedst not, neither delightedst in,—the which, according to the law, are offered,
text.DRB: In saying before, Sacrifices, and oblations, and holocausts for sin thou wouldest not, neither are they pleasing to thee, which are offered according to the law.
text.KJV: Aboue when hee said, Sacrifice, and offering, and burnt offerings, and offering for sinne thou wouldest not, neither hadst pleasure therein, which are offered by the Law:
text.NABRE: First he says, “Sacrifices and offerings, holocausts and sin offerings, you neither desired nor delighted in.” These are offered according to the law.

text.NRSV: then he added, “See, I have come to do your will.” He abolishes the first in order to establish the second.
text.Rotherham: Then, hath he said—Lo! I am come! to do, thy will:—he taketh away the first, that, the second, he may establish:
text.DRB: Then said I: Behold, I come to do thy will, O God: he taketh away the first, that he may establish that which followeth.
text.KJV: Then said he, Loe, I come to doe thy will (O God:) He taketh away the first, that he may establish the second.
text.NABRE: Then he says, “Behold, I come to do your will.” He takes away the first to establish the second.

text.NRSV: And by that will we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all.
text.Rotherham: By which will, we have been made holy, through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ, once for all.
text.DRB: In the which will, we are sanctified by the oblation of the body of Jesus Christ once.
text.KJV: By the which will wee are sanctified, through the offering of the body of Iesus Christ once for all.
text.NABRE: By this “will,” we have been consecrated through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all.

text.NRSV: And every priest stands day after day at his service, offering again and again the same sacrifices that can never take away sins.
text.Rotherham: And, every priest, indeed, standeth daily publicly ministering, and the same sacrifices ofttimes offering, the which never can clear away sins;
text.DRB: And every priest indeed standeth daily ministering, and often offering the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins.
text.KJV: And euery Priest standeth dayly ministring and offering oftentimes the same sacrifices which can neuer take away sinnes.
text.NABRE: Every priest stands daily at his ministry, offering frequently those same sacrifices that can never take away sins.

text.NRSV: But when Christ had offered for all time a single sacrifice for sins, “he sat down at the right hand of God,”
text.Rotherham: But this priest, having offered one sacrifice for sins evermore, sat down on the right hand of God:
text.DRB: But this man offering one sacrifice for sins, for ever sitteth on the right hand of God,
text.KJV: But this man after he had offered one sacrifice for sinnes for euer, sate downe on the right hand of God,
text.NABRE: But this one offered one sacrifice for sins, and took his seat forever at the right hand of God;

text.NRSV: and since then has been waiting “until his enemies would be made a footstool for his feet.”
text.Rotherham: As for the rest, waiting—until his foes be made his footstool;
text.DRB: From henceforth expecting, until his enemies be made his footstool.
text.KJV: From henceforth expecting till his enemies be made his footstoole.
text.NABRE: now he waits until his enemies are made his footstool.

text.NRSV: For by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are sanctified.
text.Rotherham: For, by one offering, hath he perfected for evermore, them who are being made holy.
text.DRB: For by one oblation he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified.
text.KJV: For by one offering hee hath perfected for euer them that are sanctified.
text.NABRE: For by one offering he has made perfect forever those who are being consecrated.

text.NRSV: And the Holy Spirit also testifies to us, for after saying,
text.Rotherham: But even the Holy Spirit beareth us witness; for, after having said—
text.DRB: And the Holy Ghost also doth testify this to us. For after that he said:
text.KJV: Whereof the holy Ghost also is a witnesse to vs: for after that he had said before,
text.NABRE: The holy Spirit also testifies to us, for after saying:

text.NRSV: “This is the covenant that I will make with them after those days, says the Lord: I will put my laws in their hearts, and I will write them on their minds,”
text.Rotherham: This is the covenant which I will covenant unto them after these days, saith the Lord,—Giving my laws upon their hearts, upon their understandings also, will I inscribe them,
text.DRB: And this is the testament which I will make unto them after those days, saith the Lord. I will give my laws in their hearts, and on their minds will I write them:
text.KJV: This is the Couenant that I wil make with them after those dayes, saith the Lord: I will put my Lawes into their hearts, and in their mindes will I write them:
text.NABRE: “This is the covenant I will establish with them after those days, says the Lord: ‘I will put my laws in their hearts, and I will write them upon their minds,’”

text.NRSV: he also says, “I will remember their sins and their lawless deeds no more.”
text.Rotherham: [He] also [saith] —of their sins, and of their lawlessnesses, I will in nowise be mindful any more.
text.DRB: And their sins and iniquities I will remember no more.
text.KJV: And their sinnes and iniquities will I remember no more.
text.NABRE: he also says: “Their sins and their evildoing I will remember no more.”

text.NRSV: Where there is forgiveness of these, there is no longer any offering for sin.
text.Rotherham: But, wherever a remission of these is, there is, no further, offering, for sins.
text.DRB: Now where there is a remission of these, there is no more an oblation for sin .
text.KJV: Now, where remission of these is, there is no more offering for sinne.
text.NABRE: Where there is forgiveness of these, there is no longer offering for sin. Recalling the Past.

text.NRSV: Therefore, my friends, since we have confidence to enter the sanctuary by the blood of Jesus,
text.Rotherham: Having therefore, brethren, freedom of speech for the entrance through the Holy place, by the blood of Jesus,
text.DRB: Having therefore, brethren, a confidence in the entering into the holies by the blood of Christ;
text.KJV: Hauing therefore, brethren, boldnesse to enter into the Holiest by the blood of Iesus,
text.NABRE: Therefore, brothers, since through the blood of Jesus we have confidence of entrance into the sanctuary

text.NRSV: by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain (that is, through his flesh),
text.Rotherham: Which entrance he hath consecrated for us, as a way recent and living, through the veil, that is, his flesh,—
text.DRB: A new and living way which he hath dedicated for us through the veil, that is to say, his flesh,
text.KJV: By a new and liuing way which hee hath consecrated for vs, through the vaile, that is to say, His flesh:
text.NABRE: by the new and living way he opened for us through the veil, that is, his flesh,

text.NRSV: and since we have a great priest over the house of God,
text.Rotherham: And having a great priest over the house of God,
text.DRB: And a high priest over the house of God:
text.KJV: And hauing an high Priest ouer the house of God:
text.NABRE: and since we have “a great priest over the house of God,”

text.NRSV: let us approach with a true heart in full assurance of faith, with our hearts sprinkled clean from an evil conscience and our bodies washed with pure water.
text.Rotherham: Let us approach with a genuine heart, in full assurance of faith, having been sprinkled, as to our hearts, from an evil conscience, and bathed, as to our bodies, with pure water;
text.DRB: Let us draw near with a true heart in fulness of faith, having our hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience, and our bodies washed with clean water.
text.KJV: Let vs drawe neere with a true heart in full assurance of faith, hauing our hearts sprinkled from an euill conscience, and our bodies washed with pure water.
text.NABRE: let us approach with a sincere heart and in absolute trust, with our hearts sprinkled clean from an evil conscience and our bodies washed in pure water.

text.NRSV: Let us hold fast to the confession of our hope without wavering, for he who has promised is faithful.
text.Rotherham: Let us hold fast the confession of the hope without wavering,—for, faithful, is he that hath promised;
text.DRB: Let us hold fast the confession of our hope without wavering (for he is faithful that hath promised),
text.KJV: Let vs hold fast the profession of our faith without wauering (for he is faithfull that promised)
text.NABRE: Let us hold unwaveringly to our confession that gives us hope, for he who made the promise is trustworthy.

text.NRSV: And let us consider how to provoke one another to love and good deeds,
text.Rotherham: And let us attentively consider one another, to provoke unto love and noble works,—
text.DRB: And let us consider one another, to provoke unto charity and to good works:
text.KJV: And let vs consider one another to prouoke vnto loue, and to good workes:
text.NABRE: We must consider how to rouse one another to love and good works.

text.NRSV: not neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another, and all the more as you see the Day approaching.
text.Rotherham: Not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, according to the custom of some, but exhorting, and by so much the more as this, by as much as ye behold, the day, drawing near.
text.DRB: Not forsaking our assembly, as some are accustomed; but comforting one another, and so much the more as you see the day approaching.
text.KJV: Not forsaking the assembling of our selues together, as the manner of some is: but exhorting one another, and so much the more, as ye see the day approching.
text.NABRE: We should not stay away from our assembly, as is the custom of some, but encourage one another, and this all the more as you see the day drawing near.

text.NRSV: For if we willfully persist in sin after having received the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins,
text.Rotherham: For, if, by choice, we be sinning, after the receiving of the full-knowledge of the truth, no longer, for sins, is there left over, a sacrifice,
text.DRB: For if we sin wilfully after having the knowledge of the truth, there is now left no sacrifice for sins,
text.KJV: For if we sinne wilfully after that we haue receiued the knowledge of the trueth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sinnes,
text.NABRE: If we sin deliberately after receiving knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains sacrifice for sins

text.NRSV: but a fearful prospect of judgment, and a fury of fire that will consume the adversaries.
text.Rotherham: But some fearful reception of judgment and fiery jealousy, about to devour the opposers.
text.DRB: But a certain dreadful expectation of judgment, and the rage of a fire which shall consume the adversaries.
text.KJV: But a certaine fearefull looking for of iudgement, and fiery indignation, which shall deuoure the aduersaries.
text.NABRE: but a fearful prospect of judgment and a flaming fire that is going to consume the adversaries.

text.NRSV: Anyone who has violated the law of Moses dies without mercy “on the testimony of two or three witnesses.”
text.Rotherham: Any one having set aside a law of Moses, apart from compassions, upon [the testimony of] two or three witnesses, dieth:
text.DRB: A man making void the law of Moses, dieth without any mercy under two or three witnesses:
text.KJV: Hee that despised Moses Lawe, died without mercy, vnder two or three witnesses.
text.NABRE: Anyone who rejects the law of Moses is put to death without pity on the testimony of two or three witnesses.

text.NRSV: How much worse punishment do you think will be deserved by those who have spurned the Son of God, profaned the blood of the covenant by which they were sanctified, and outraged the Spirit of grace?
text.Rotherham: Of how much sorer punishment, suppose ye, shall he be accounted worthy, who hath trampled underfoot the Son of God, and, the blood of the covenant, hath esteemed, a profane thing, by which he had been made holy, and, unto the Spirit of favour, hath offered wanton insult?
text.DRB: How much more, do you think he deserveth worse punishments, who hath trodden under foot the Son of God, and hath esteemed the blood of the testament unclean, by which he was sanctified, and hath offered an affront to the Spirit of grace?
text.KJV: Of how much sorer punishment suppose ye, shall hee be thought worthy, who hath troden vnder foote þ Sonne of God, and hath counted the blood of the couenant wherwith he was sanctified, an vnholy thing, and hath done despite vnto the spirit of grace?
text.NABRE: Do you not think that a much worse punishment is due the one who has contempt for the Son of God, considers unclean the covenant-blood by which he was consecrated, and insults the spirit of grace?

text.NRSV: For we know the one who said, “Vengeance is mine, I will repay.” And again, “The Lord will judge his people.”
text.Rotherham: For we know him that hath said—To me, belongeth avenging, I, will recompense; and again—The Lord will judge his people.
text.DRB: For we know him that hath said: Vengeance belongeth to me, and I will repay. And again: The Lord shall judge his people.
text.KJV: For we know him that hath said, Uengeance belongeth vnto me, I wil recompence, saith the Lord: and again, The Lord shall iudge his people.
text.NABRE: We know the one who said: “Vengeance is mine; I will repay,” and again: “The Lord will judge his people.”

text.NRSV: It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God.
text.Rotherham: A fearful thing [it is] to fall into the hands of a Living God!
text.DRB: It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God.
text.KJV: It is a fearefull thing to fall into the hands of the liuing God.
text.NABRE: It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God.

text.NRSV: But recall those earlier days when, after you had been enlightened, you endured a hard struggle with sufferings,
text.Rotherham: But be calling to mind the former days, in which, once ye were illuminated, a great combat of sufferings, ye endured;—
text.DRB: But call to mind the former days, wherein, being illuminated, you endured a great fight of afflictions.
text.KJV: But call to remembrance the former dayes, in which after yee were illuminated, ye indured a great fight of afflictions:
text.NABRE: Remember the days past when, after you had been enlightened, you endured a great contest of suffering.

text.NRSV: sometimes being publicly exposed to abuse and persecution, and sometimes being partners with those so treated.
text.Rotherham: Partly, indeed, because, both with reproaches and tribulations, ye were being made a spectacle, but, partly, because, into fellowship with them who were so involved, ye were brought;—
text.DRB: And on the one hand indeed, by reproaches and tribulations, were made a gazingstock; and on the other, became companions of them that were used in such sort.
text.KJV: Partly whilest ye were made a gazing stocke both by reproches & afflictions, and partly whilest ye became companions of them that were so vsed.
text.NABRE: At times you were publicly exposed to abuse and affliction; at other times you associated yourselves with those so treated.

text.NRSV: For you had compassion on those who were in prison, and you cheerfully accepted the plundering of your possessions, knowing that you yourselves possessed something better and more lasting.
text.Rotherham: For, even with them who were in bonds, ye sympathised, and, unto the seizure of your goods, with joy, ye bade welcome,—knowing that ye have yourselves, for a better possession and an abiding.
text.DRB: For you both had compassion on them that were in bands, and took with joy the being stripped of your own goods, knowing that you have a better and a lasting substance.
text.KJV: For yee had compassion of me in my bonds, and tooke ioyfully the spoyling of your goods, knowing in your selues that yee haue in heauen a better and an induring substance.
text.NABRE: You even joined in the sufferings of those in prison and joyfully accepted the confiscation of your property, knowing that you had a better and lasting possession.

text.NRSV: Do not, therefore, abandon that confidence of yours; it brings a great reward.
text.Rotherham: Do not, then, cast away your freedom of speech,—the which hath a great recompense.
text.DRB: Do not therefore lose your confidence, which hath a great reward.
text.KJV: Cast not away therfore your confidence which hath great recompense of reward.
text.NABRE: Therefore, do not throw away your confidence; it will have great recompense.

text.NRSV: For you need endurance, so that when you have done the will of God, you may receive what was promised.
text.Rotherham: For, of endurance, ye have need, in order that, the will of God having done, Ye may bear away, the promise.
text.DRB: For patience is necessary for you; that, doing the will of God, you may receive the promise.
text.KJV: For ye haue need of patience, that shall after ye haue done the will of God ye might receiue the promise.
text.NABRE: You need endurance to do the will of God and receive what he has promised.

text.NRSV: For yet “in a very little while, the one who is coming will come and will not delay;
text.Rotherham: For, yet a little while, how short! how short! The Coming One will be here, and will not tarry;
text.DRB: For yet a little and a very little while, and he that is to come, will come, and will not delay.
text.KJV: For yet a litle while, and he that shall come will come, and will not tary.
text.NABRE: “For, after just a brief moment, he who is to come shall come; he shall not delay.

text.NRSV: but my righteous one will live by faith. My soul takes no pleasure in anyone who shrinks back.”
text.Rotherham: But, my righteous one, by faith, shall live, and, if he draw back, my soul delighteth not in him.
text.DRB: But my just man liveth by faith; but if he withdraw himself, he shall not please my soul.
text.KJV: Now the iust shall liue by faith: but if any man drawe backe, my soule shall haue no pleasure in him.
text.NABRE: But my just one shall live by faith, and if he draws back I take no pleasure in him.”

text.NRSV: But we are not among those who shrink back and so are lost, but among those who have faith and so are saved.
text.Rotherham: We, however, are not of a drawing back unto destruction, but, of faith, unto an acquisition of life.
text.DRB: But we are not the children of withdrawing unto perdition, but of faith to the saving of the soul.
text.KJV: But wee are not of them who draw backe vnto perdition: but of them that beleeue, to the sauing of the soule.
text.NABRE: We are not among those who draw back and perish, but among those who have faith and will possess life.

text.NRSV: Now faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.
text.Rotherham: But faith is, of things hoped for, a confidence, of facts, a conviction, when they are not seen;
text.DRB: Now faith is the substance of things to be hoped for, the evidence of things that appear not.
text.KJV: Now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the euidence of things not seen.
text.NABRE: V. Examples, Discipline, Disobedience Chapter 11 Faith of the Ancients. Faith is the realization of what is hoped for and evidence of things not seen.

text.NRSV: Indeed, by it our ancestors received approval.
text.Rotherham: For, thereby, well-attested were the ancients.
text.DRB: For by this the ancients obtained a testimony.
text.KJV: For by it the Elders obtained a good report.
text.NABRE: Because of it the ancients were well attested.

text.NRSV: By faith we understand that the worlds were prepared by the word of God, so that what is seen was made from things that are not visible.
text.Rotherham: By faith, we understand the ages to have been fitted together, by declaration of God,—to the end that, not out of things appearing, should that which is seen, have come into existence.
text.DRB: By faith we understand that the world was framed by the word of God; that from invisible things visible things might be made.
text.KJV: Through faith we vnderstand that the worlds were framed by the word of God, so that things which are seene were not made of things which doe appeare.
text.NABRE: By faith we understand that the universe was ordered by the word of God, so that what is visible came into being through the invisible.

text.NRSV: By faith Abel offered to God a more acceptable sacrifice than Cain’s. Through this he received approval as righteous, God himself giving approval to his gifts; he died, but through his faith he still speaks.
text.Rotherham: By faith, a fuller sacrifice, did Abel, offer unto God, than Cain,—through which, he received witness that he was righteous, there being a witnessing upon his gifts, by God; and, through it, though he died, he yet is speaking.
text.DRB: By faith Abel offered to God a sacrifice exceeding that of Cain, by which he obtained a testimony that he was just, God giving testimony to his gifts; and by it he being dead yet speaketh.
text.KJV: By faith Abel offered vnto God a more excellent sacrifice then Kain, by which he obtained witnes that he was righteous, God testifying of his gifts: and by it he being dead, yet speaketh.
text.NABRE: By faith Abel offered to God a sacrifice greater than Cain’s. Through this he was attested to be righteous, God bearing witness to his gifts, and through this, though dead, he still speaks.

text.NRSV: By faith Enoch was taken so that he did not experience death; and “he was not found, because God had taken him.” For it was attested before he was taken away that “he had pleased God.”
text.Rotherham: By faith, Enoch was translated, so as not to see death, and was not found, because that, God, had translated him; for, before the translation, he had received witness that he had become well-pleasing unto God;—
text.DRB: By faith Henoch was translated, that he should not see death; and he was not found, because God had translated him: for before his translation he had testimony that he pleased God.
text.KJV: By faith Enoch was translated, that he should not see death, and was not found, because God had translated him: For before his translation he had this testimonie, that he pleased God.
text.NABRE: By faith Enoch was taken up so that he should not see death, and “he was found no more because God had taken him.” Before he was taken up, he was attested to have pleased God.

text.NRSV: And without faith it is impossible to please God, for whoever would approach him must believe that he exists and that he rewards those who seek him.
text.Rotherham: But, apart from faith, it is impossible to be well-pleasing; for he that approacheth unto God—must needs have faith, that he is, and that, to them who seek him out, a rewarder he becometh.
text.DRB: But without faith it is impossible to please God. For he that cometh to God, must believe that he is, and is a rewarder to them that seek him.
text.KJV: But without faith it is impossible to please him: for hee that commeth to God, must beleeue that he is, and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seeke him.
text.NABRE: But without faith it is impossible to please him, for anyone who approaches God must believe that he exists and that he rewards those who seek him.

text.NRSV: By faith Noah, warned by God about events as yet unseen, respected the warning and built an ark to save his household; by this he condemned the world and became an heir to the righteousness that is in accordance with faith.
text.Rotherham: By faith, Noah, having received intimation concerning the things not yet seen, filled with reverence, prepared an ark to the saving of his house—through which he condemned the world, and, of the righteousness by way of faith, became heir.
text.DRB: By faith Noe, having received an answer concerning those things which as yet were not seen, moved with fear, framed the ark for the saving of his house, by the which he condemned the world; and was instituted heir of the justice which is by faith.
text.KJV: By faith Noah being warned of God of things not seene as yet, moued with feare, prepared an Arke to the sauing of his house, by the which he condemned the world, and became heire of the righteousnesse which is by faith.
text.NABRE: By faith Noah, warned about what was not yet seen, with reverence built an ark for the salvation of his household. Through this he condemned the world and inherited the righteousness that comes through faith.

text.NRSV: By faith Abraham obeyed when he was called to set out for a place that he was to receive as an inheritance; and he set out, not knowing where he was going.
text.Rotherham: By faith, being called, Abraham obeyed—to come forth into a place he was destined to receive for an inheritance; and he came forth, not well knowing whither he was coming.
text.DRB: By faith he that is called Abraham , obeyed to go out into a place which he was to receive for an inheritance; and he went out, not knowing whither he went.
text.KJV: By faith Abraham when he was called to goe out into a place which hee should after receiue for an inheritance, obeyed, and he went out, not knowing whither he went.
text.NABRE: By faith Abraham obeyed when he was called to go out to a place that he was to receive as an inheritance; he went out, not knowing where he was to go.

text.NRSV: By faith he stayed for a time in the land he had been promised, as in a foreign land, living in tents, as did Isaac and Jacob, who were heirs with him of the same promise.
text.Rotherham: By faith, he sojourned in the land of promise, as a foreign land, in tents, dwelling, along with Isaac and Jacob, the joint-heirs of the same promise;
text.DRB: By faith he abode in the land, dwelling in cottages, with Isaac and Jacob, the co-heirs of the same promise.
text.KJV: By faith hee soiourned in the land of promise, as in a strange countrey, dwelling in tabernacles with Isaac and Iacob, the heires with him of the same promise.
text.NABRE: By faith he sojourned in the promised land as in a foreign country, dwelling in tents with Isaac and Jacob, heirs of the same promise;

text.NRSV: For he looked forward to the city that has foundations, whose architect and builder is God.
text.Rotherham: For he was awaiting the city having foundations, whose architect and builder is, God.
text.DRB: For he looked for a city that hath foundations; whose builder and maker is God.
text.KJV: For hee looked for a citie which hath foundations, whose builder and maker is God.
text.NABRE: for he was looking forward to the city with foundations, whose architect and maker is God.

text.NRSV: By faith he received power of procreation, even though he was too old—and Sarah herself was barren—because he considered him faithful who had promised.
text.Rotherham: By faith, even Sarah herself, received power for founding a seed, even beyond the season of life’s prime,—seeing that, faithful, she reckoned, him that had promised;
text.DRB: By faith also Sara herself, being barren, received strength to conceive seed, even past the time of age; because she believed that he was faithful who had promised,
text.KJV: Through faith also Sara her selfe receiued strength to conceiue seede, and was deliuered of a child when she was past age, because she iudged him faithful who had promised.
text.NABRE: By faith he received power to generate, even though he was past the normal age—and Sarah herself was sterile—for he thought that the one who had made the promise was trustworthy.

text.NRSV: Therefore from one person, and this one as good as dead, descendants were born, “as many as the stars of heaven and as the innumerable grains of sand by the seashore.”
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, even from one, were born, and, as to these things, one who had become dead,—like the stars of the heaven, for multitude, and as the sand that is by the lip of the sea, that cannot be numbered.
text.DRB: For which cause there sprung even from one (and him as good as dead) as the stars of heaven in multitude, and as the sand which is by the sea shore innumerable.
text.KJV: Therfore sprang there euen of one, and him as good as dead, so many as the starres of the skie in multitude, and as the sand which is by the sea shore innumerable.
text.NABRE: So it was that there came forth from one man, himself as good as dead, descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky and as countless as the sands on the seashore.

text.NRSV: All of these died in faith without having received the things promised, but from a distance they saw them and greeted them. They confessed that they were strangers and foreigners on the earth,
text.Rotherham: In faith, all these died—not bearing away the promises, but, from afar, beholding and saluting them, and confessing that, strangers and sojourners, were they upon the land.
text.DRB: All these died according to faith, not having received the promises, but beholding them afar off, and saluting them, and confessing that they are pilgrims and strangers on the earth.
text.KJV: These all died in faith, not hauing receiued the promises, but hauing seene them a farre off, and were perswaded of them, and embraced them, and confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth.
text.NABRE: All these died in faith. They did not receive what had been promised but saw it and greeted it from afar and acknowledged themselves to be strangers and aliens on earth,

text.NRSV: for people who speak in this way make it clear that they are seeking a homeland.
text.Rotherham: For, they who such things as these are saying, make it clear that, of a paternal home they are in quest;
text.DRB: For they that say these things, do signify that they seek a country.
text.KJV: For they that say such things, declare plainly that they seeke a countrey.
text.NABRE: for those who speak thus show that they are seeking a homeland.

text.NRSV: If they had been thinking of the land that they had left behind, they would have had opportunity to return.
text.Rotherham: And, if indeed of that they had been mindful, from which they had come out, they might, in that case, have had an opportunity, to return;
text.DRB: And truly if they had been mindful of that from whence they came out, they had doubtless time to return.
text.KJV: And truly if they had been mindfull of that countrey, from whence they came out, they might haue had opportunitie to haue returned:
text.NABRE: If they had been thinking of the land from which they had come, they would have had opportunity to return.

text.NRSV: But as it is, they desire a better homeland, that is, a heavenly one. Therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God; indeed, he has prepared a city for them.
text.Rotherham: But, now, after a better one, are they reaching, that is, a heavenly; wherefore God is not ashamed of them, to be invoked as, their God,—for he hath prepared for them, a city.
text.DRB: But now they desire a better, that is to say, a heavenly country. Therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God; for he hath prepared for them a city.
text.KJV: But now they desire a better countrey, that is, an heauenly: wherefore God is not ashamed to bee called their God: for he hath prepared for thē a city.
text.NABRE: But now they desire a better homeland, a heavenly one. Therefore, God is not ashamed to be called their God, for he has prepared a city for them.

text.NRSV: By faith Abraham, when put to the test, offered up Isaac. He who had received the promises was ready to offer up his only son,
text.Rotherham: By faith, Abraham, when tested, offered up Isaac, and, the only-begotten, would he have offered up, who the promises had accepted,—
text.DRB: By faith Abraham, when he was tried, offered Isaac: and he that had received the promises, offered up his only begotten son;
text.KJV: By faith Abraham when he was tried, offered vp Isaac: and he that had receiued the promises, offered vp his onely begotten sonne,
text.NABRE: By faith Abraham, when put to the test, offered up Isaac, and he who had received the promises was ready to offer his only son,

text.NRSV: of whom he had been told, “It is through Isaac that descendants shall be named for you.”
text.Rotherham: Even him of whom it had been said—In Isaac, shall there be called to thee, a seed:
text.DRB: (To whom it was said: In Isaac shall thy seed be called.)
text.KJV: Of whom it was said, That, in Isaac shall thy seed be called:
text.NABRE: of whom it was said, “Through Isaac descendants shall bear your name.”

text.NRSV: He considered the fact that God is able even to raise someone from the dead—and figuratively speaking, he did receive him back.
text.Rotherham: Accounting that, even from among the dead, God, was able, to raise [him],—whence, even in similitude, he bare him away.
text.DRB: Accounting that God is able to raise up even from the dead. Whereupon also he received him for a parable .
text.KJV: Accounting that God was able to raise him vp, euen from the dead: from whence also he receiued him in a figure.
text.NABRE: He reasoned that God was able to raise even from the dead, and he received Isaac back as a symbol.

text.NRSV: By faith Isaac invoked blessings for the future on Jacob and Esau.
text.Rotherham: By faith, even concerning things to come, did Isaac bless Jacob and Esau.
text.DRB: By faith also of things to come, Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau.
text.KJV: By faith Isaac blessed Iacob and Esau concerning things to come.
text.NABRE: By faith regarding things still to come Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau.

text.NRSV: By faith Jacob, when dying, blessed each of the sons of Joseph, “bowing in worship over the top of his staff.”
text.Rotherham: By faith, Jacob, when about to die, blessed each of the sons of Joseph; and bowed in worship on the top of his staff.
text.DRB: By faith Jacob dying, blessed each of the sons of Joseph, and adored the top of his rod .
text.KJV: By faith Iacob when hee was a dying, blessed both the sonnes of Ioseph, and worshipped leaning vpon the top of his staffe.
text.NABRE: By faith Jacob, when dying, blessed each of the sons of Joseph and “bowed in worship, leaning on the top of his staff.”

text.NRSV: By faith Joseph, at the end of his life, made mention of the exodus of the Israelites and gave instructions about his burial.
text.Rotherham: By faith, Joseph, when drawing to his end—concerning the exodus of the sons of Israel, called to remembrance, and, concerning his bones, gave commandment.
text.DRB: By faith Joseph, when he was dying, made mention of the going out of the children of Israel; and gave commandment concerning his bones.
text.KJV: By faith, Ioseph when hee died, made mention of the departing of the children of Israel: and gaue commandement concerning his bones.
text.NABRE: By faith Joseph, near the end of his life, spoke of the Exodus of the Israelites and gave instructions about his bones.

text.NRSV: By faith Moses was hidden by his parents for three months after his birth, because they saw that the child was beautiful; and they were not afraid of the king’s edict.
text.Rotherham: By faith, Moses, when born—was hid three months by his parents, because, they saw, that, goodly, was, the child, and were not affrighted, at the decree of the king.
text.DRB: By faith Moses, when he was born, was hid three months by his parents; because they saw he was a comely babe, and they feared not the king's edict.
text.KJV: By faith; Moses when hee was borne was hid three moneths of his parents, because they saw he was a proper childe, and they not afraid of the Kings commandement.
text.NABRE: By faith Moses was hidden by his parents for three months after his birth, because they saw that he was a beautiful child, and they were not afraid of the king’s edict.

text.NRSV: By faith Moses, when he was grown up, refused to be called a son of Pharaoh’s daughter,
text.Rotherham: By faith, Moses, when grown up—refused to be called the son of a daughter of a Pharaoh,
text.DRB: By faith Moses, when he was grown up, denied himself to be the son of Pharao's daughter;
text.KJV: By faith Moses when hee was come to yeeres, refused to bee called the sonne of Pharaohs daughter,
text.NABRE: By faith Moses, when he had grown up, refused to be known as the son of Pharaoh’s daughter;

text.NRSV: choosing rather to share ill-treatment with the people of God than to enjoy the fleeting pleasures of sin.
text.Rotherham: Rather choosing—to be jointly suffering ill-treatment with the people of God, than, for a season, to be having, sins enjoyment;
text.DRB: Rather choosing to be afflicted with the people of God, than to have the pleasure of sin for a time,
